categories.name: Business Systems
categories.description: The unglamorous mechanics that make growth repeatable.

articles.title: This Is How You Create Software People Will Beg to Pay For
articles.excerpt: Most SaaS founders ask ‘Would you use this?’ — one of the least useful questions in customer discovery. Here’s a five-question framework that finds out if the problem is worth paying to solve.
articles.content:
I learned this from a SaaS founder who failed at three startups before he finally discovered the antidote. Not the antidote to failure. The antidote to building software nobody wants. And it's surprisingly simple.

By the end of this article, you'll learn a customer discovery framework that helps you validate whether people have a problem worth solving before you spend months building software they were never going to pay for. Let's dive in.

The fat mistake most SaaS founders make. Man gets an idea. Man spends weeks—or months—or even years—building it. Man finishes. Launch day cometh. Then silence. No customers. No traction. No revenue.

The assumption is usually that the product wasn't good enough. But more often, the real problem happened long before a single line of code was written because the founder never properly validated the problem. Or worse—they asked the wrong questions.

The question that lies to you. Most aspiring founders ask something like this in their customer discovery surveys: 'Would you use this?' Sounds reasonable, huh? It's also one of the least useful questions you can ask.

Why? Because you're asking someone to predict their future behavior. Humans are notoriously bad at doing that. Saying, 'Yes, I'd probably use it,' costs nothing. People want to be helpful. They want to encourage you. They imagine themselves using your product under ideal circumstances. Then real life gets in the way.

That's how founders end up with dozens of positive survey responses… but few—or even zero—paying customers.

Ask about the past, not the future. Instead of asking what people might do, ask what they're already doing. Past behavior is far more reliable than future intentions. People may misjudge what they'll do next month, but they know exactly how they solved the problem yesterday. That's where the real insights live.

The five-question customer discovery system. Every customer conversation in your survey should uncover one thing: is this problem painful enough that people will actually pay to solve it? These five questions help you find out.

1. 'How do you solve this today?' This reveals your real competition. It usually isn't another startup. It's spreadsheets, sticky notes, WhatsApp, email, manual work, or simply doing nothing. You can't position yourself effectively until you know what you're replacing. Make sense?

2. 'What's the most frustrating part?' This is where your marketing begins. Pay close attention to the exact words people use. Those words often become your headlines, landing pages, advertisements, and sales copy. Don't rewrite their language. Borrow it.

3. 'What have you already tried?' This separates people actively looking for a solution from people who simply enjoy complaining. Someone who's already spent time, money, or effort trying to solve the problem is much more likely to become a paying customer. Intent matters.

4. 'What does the ideal solution look like?' Notice you're not asking which features they want. You're asking about the outcome they actually care about. Customers rarely buy software because it has Feature A or Feature B. They buy software because they want a result.

5. 'What does this problem cost you?' This is the most important question of all. Does the problem cost them money? Time? Customers? Productivity? Stress? Lost opportunities? If someone can't clearly explain what the problem is costing them, there's a good chance it isn't painful enough for them to pay to solve. Pain creates urgency. Urgency drives purchases.

The pattern you're looking for. Don't stop after five conversations—or even ten. Aim for at least 25 customer interviews. Somewhere around conversations 15 to 20, something interesting usually happens. You stop hearing new problems. Instead, the same frustrations appear again. The same workarounds keep coming up. The same words get repeated. That's your signal. You're no longer listening to individual opinions. You're uncovering a pattern. And patterns are what businesses are built on.

Don't just copy the tactic. Understand the SYSTEM. The real lesson isn't to memorize five questions. It's to understand why they work. Founders often fall in love with their solution before proving the problem exists. Customer discovery surveys reverse that process. Instead of asking people to validate your idea, you're validating the problem itself. Once you know the problem is real—and painful—you can build with far greater confidence that people will actually pay for what you create.

That's the difference between building software people say they want… and building software people genuinely want badly enough to buy.

Your turn. Schedule five customer conversations this week. Ask these five questions in order. Resist the temptation to pitch your idea. Just listen. Then repeat the process until you've completed at least 25 conversations. If you keep hearing the same frustrations, the same workarounds, and the same language, you're probably onto something worth building.

articles.title: One Call to Action Beats Five Every Time
articles.excerpt: A WordStream study found emails with a single, focused call to action generate up to 371% more clicks and 1,617% more sales than emails with multiple competing CTAs.
articles.content:
A WordStream study found that emails with a single, focused call to action generate up to 371% more clicks and 1,617% more sales than emails with multiple competing CTAs. At first glance, that statistic almost sounds too good to be true. Surely giving people more options should increase the chances they'll choose one… right?

By the end of this article, you'll learn a quick, simple, and actionable technique you can apply immediately to recover some of the sales opportunities you've been losing because of this one common mistake.

Ironically, one of the easiest ways to quietly sabotage your marketing is by asking people to do too many things at once. You finish an email and write: 'Book a call.' 'Follow us on Instagram.' 'Check out our website.' 'Reply to this email.' 'Download our free guide.' It feels productive because you've covered all your bases. But from your customer's perspective, you've created a decision-making problem.

Instead of making one simple, clear decision, they now have to decide which action to take. That tiny moment of hesitation is often enough for them to do… nothing. That's exactly why a WordStream analysis found that emails with a single, focused call to action significantly outperformed emails containing multiple competing CTAs, generating substantially more clicks and sales. The takeaway isn't that multiple CTAs never work—it's that clarity often wins over quantity.

More options don't always mean more action. Imagine you walk into a restaurant and the menu contains 300 different meals. Sounds great… until you actually have to choose. You spend several minutes comparing options, second-guessing yourself, and wondering if you're making the right choice.

Now compare that with a restaurant famous for one signature dish. The decision practically disappears.

Marketing works in a similar way. Every additional call to action—especially at the same point in a piece of copy—competes for your customer's attention. Instead of increasing the likelihood they'll act, you're often dividing their focus.

This is a well-known psychological principle called choice overload. When people are presented with too many competing options, they're more likely to delay making a decision—or avoid making one altogether.

The real system. This isn't really about having one button instead of three. It's about understanding where your customer is inside your sales system.

Someone discovering your business for the first time probably shouldn't receive the same call to action as someone who's already requested a quote. Every stage of your sales funnel has one action that matters most.

Your job isn't to ask for everything. Your job is to identify the next step. That's the system.

Here's what to do today. Go back through your recent emails, DMs, newsletters, or sales messages. Find every message where you've asked people to do more than one thing.

Then ask yourself: 'At this stage of my sales funnel, what's the single most important action I want this customer to take?' Keep that one. Delete everything else.

If you're trying to generate consultations, make the entire message about booking the consultation. If you're trying to grow your email list, make the entire message about joining the list. If you're trying to close a sale, make the entire message about buying.

One message. One objective. One call to action. Now resend the updated copy. Easy peasy.

Don't just copy the tactic—understand the SYSTEM. The real lesson is that every piece of marketing should move your customer one step forward—not present five different paths.

Your sales funnel already tells you what that next step should be. So when your message asks for anything beyond that, you're introducing unnecessary friction.

The businesses that convert well don't just write better emails. They build systems that remove unnecessary decisions.

Your turn. Take your last five emails or DMs. If any of them contain multiple calls to action, rewrite them so they contain only one—the one that best matches where that customer is in your sales funnel.

Then resend them where appropriate and compare the response rate.

articles.title: Stop Asking for Referrals at the Ugliest Possible Moment
articles.excerpt: Most business owners know just how valuable referrals are. Yet surprisingly few have a working system for consistently getting them.
articles.content:
Stop asking for referrals at the ugliest possible moment. It's crippling your response rate.

Most business owners know just how valuable referrals are. Yet surprisingly few have a working system for consistently getting them. So, they ask whenever they happen to remember: at the checkout counter, at the end of a project, or three days later in a follow-up email. Sometimes they don't ask at all because it feels awkward. Talk about leaving money on the table.

Here's the problem: if you're confused about how to ask for referrals, or you're asking but not getting great results, it's because it's not about how to ask but rather when to ask. The right moment to ask for referrals is short and dynamic but by the end of this article, you'll learn to easily identify it and integrate that moment into your system so that you stop asking at ugly moments that cripple your response rate.

There's only one of you. Entrepreneur Alex Hormozi puts it simply: 'There are two ways people can find out about your stuff. One, you can tell them. Two, other people can tell them. And there's only one of you.'

Simple idea, but it highlights an important reality. You can only reach so many people yourself. Referrals multiply your reach because your happy customers become an extension of your marketing. Instead of one person spreading the word, you suddenly have dozens or eventually hundreds or even thousands of people doing it for you... For bonuses or... drumroll... for FREE.

You could incentivize referrals by offering bonuses, discounts, etc. Example (Software): Invite a friend. Your friend gets 30% off their first 3 months, and you receive one free month added to your subscription for every friend who becomes a paying customer. Example (Online Coaching Program): Know someone who wants to grow their business? Refer them to our coaching program. If they enroll, they receive our $197 Sales Script Pack for free, and you receive a private 1-hour strategy session (worth $300). You get the idea.

However, in this article, our focus is on getting referrals for FREE without offering any bonus, discount, or incentive and still achieving phenomenal results. So it's all about identifying that brief but powerful moment we're about to break down.

Think about the last time you watched an incredible movie. The moment you walked out of the cinema, you probably couldn't wait to tell someone about it. You were excited. The emotions were fresh. Now imagine someone asks you six weeks later. You may still recommend it but the excitement has faded. You're far less enthusiastic than you were immediately after watching it. Customers behave the same way. The emotional peak doesn't last forever, and once it's gone, you've missed your best opportunity.

The window you're overlooking. Pay attention to the moments your customers say something positive without you asking. Maybe they send you a message saying: 'This is amazing.' 'This solved my problem.' 'Thank you so much.' 'I'm really impressed.' Those comments are more valuable than you realize. Most business owners only see them as testimonials (which are very important, yes). But they also signal that your customer is experiencing peak satisfaction right now.

That's your window! Not at checkout because it's part of your routine. Not in a generic follow-up email three days later. Not after the excitement has cooled off. Right then! Because in that moment, recommending you to someone else feels natural instead of forced.

Nothing complicated. A simple response is often enough: 'I'm really glad it's working for you. Do you know one or two people who'd find this useful as well?' Notice how different that feels from randomly asking everyone for referrals? You're responding to genuine enthusiasm instead of trying to create it.

Hammer it into your system. Create a simple rule: Whenever a customer gives unsolicited positive feedback, ask for a referral. Every single time. No guessing. No relying on memory. No wondering whether this customer might be annoyed. You've created a trigger. Just like your business has a system for sending invoices or following up with leads, build this system for free referrals. Once the trigger exists, consistency becomes automatic. And consistency is what produces results.

Why this compounds. Imagine you have 100 satisfied customers. If each of them referred just two friends over time, that's 200 potential new customers without spending additional money on advertising. Of course, not every referral will become a customer. Not every customer will refer exactly two people either. The point isn't the exact number it's the multiplication effect. Every satisfied customer has the potential to introduce your business to people who already trust the recommendation because it came from someone they know.

That's one reason referrals are often among the highest-quality leads a business can receive- they arrive with a level of trust that's difficult to create through advertising alone. Research has consistently found that referred customers tend to convert at higher rates and often deliver greater long-term value than customers acquired through many other channels. Nielsen has repeatedly found that recommendations from people we know are among the most trusted forms of advertising, while research from the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania has shown that referred customers can provide greater long-term value than non-referred customers. That's leverage baby!

Key takeaway: People are most willing to recommend you when they're already feeling the value you've created. So don't wait until the moment has passed. Build a simple system: Every time a customer gives you genuine, unsolicited positive feedback, let that become your trigger to ask.

articles.title: Sell the Status, Not the Product
articles.excerpt: If you sell luxury goods, style, or anything that doesn't solve a painfully obvious problem, here's the reframe that changes your marketing.
articles.content:
Never ever sell what your product actually does. If you sell luxury goods, style, or anything that doesn't solve a painfully obvious problem, here's a DIAMOND hack that will level up your marketing.

One of the biggest mistakes businesses make is trying to sell what their product does instead of what their customer actually wants.

You've probably heard this line somewhere before: 'People don't buy products, they buy solutions or outcomes.'

Maybe it made sense to you, maybe it didn't. Regardless, I bet you were left with more questions than answers. Or left without actionable steps to actually reframe your advertisement in a way that improves conversion.

By the end of this article, you will know your exact next step—which will take you barely a minute to complete, and you will immediately understand how it will improve your marketing, leading to more sales.

Let's dig in!

In most cases, people ultimately spend money on 3 things: Solutions, Convenience, and Experience. Here are 2 examples of each.

Solutions: Dish soap—you don't care about soap itself, but it solves the problem of dirty dishes. Investing—solves the problems of inflation, wanting to grow wealth, wanting to make more money without working more hours.

Make sense?

Convenience: Netflix—you're paying for the convenience of not driving to a store, waiting in line, or dealing with late fees. You're buying entertainment that's available instantly, from the comfort of your couch. Your maid—a clean home and chores done, without the inconvenience and time spent doing it yourself.

Make sense?

Now, solutions and convenience are obvious, but the third is tricky and often the hardest category to market.

If you're selling luxury goods, fashion, accessories, premium brands, or anything where the value isn't immediately obvious, understanding this distinction will completely change how you market your business.

People don't always buy luxury products because they solve a practical problem. They buy them because of how those products make them feel and how they influence the way other people perceive them.

That feeling is the experience: status.

That's where many businesses struggle. Because unlike practical products, the real value isn't always visible.

Here's the hack.

Open ChatGPT and paste this prompt: 'I sell [your product]. Describe how using this product elevates the status of my customer—through the customer's own perspective, and through the perspective of various people around them: friends, family, colleagues, strangers, romantic interests, social media, etc.'

In less than a minute, you'll have marketing angles you may never have considered.

ChatGPT isn't magically writing your ads for you—it's forcing you to think beyond the product and focus on the social outcomes your customer will actually buy.

Let's look at two examples.

Example 1: Premium Sneakers. Here's an excerpt from ChatGPT's response.

The customer's internal perspective: 'I look put together. I don't have to worry about whether my outfit works anymore.' 'I feel more confident walking into a room because I know I look good.' 'People take me more seriously when I dress well.' 'I'm finally dressing like the person I want to become, not the person I used to be.' 'When I catch my reflection, I actually like what I see.' 'These don't feel like just shoes—they complete my whole look.' 'I don't feel underdressed around successful people anymore.' 'I worked hard for my money. This is something I'm proud to own.'

Friends' perspective: 'Bro, those sneakers are clean.' 'You've been upgrading your style lately.' 'Where did you get those?' 'You're always wearing something nice.' 'You actually know how to dress.' Friends begin seeing the customer as someone with better taste, someone who stays current, or someone whose opinion on fashion carries more weight.

Family's perspective. Parents may think: 'He's taking better care of himself.' 'She's becoming more confident.' Siblings may think: 'You've really stepped up your style.' Relatives may notice: 'You always look sharp whenever we see you.' The sneakers become evidence that the customer is investing in themselves.

Colleagues' perspective. Coworkers might think: 'He presents himself well.' 'She has good attention to detail.' 'He looks professional even when dressing casually.' This subtle improvement can contribute to perceptions of competence and confidence, especially in workplaces where appearance influences first impressions.

Strangers' perspective. Someone walking past might think: 'Nice shoes.' 'That outfit is put together.' 'That person has style.' Even if nobody says anything, the customer often imagines these impressions, which reinforces confidence.

Romantic interest's perspective. A potential partner may think: 'They clearly take care of themselves.' 'They have good taste.' 'They're confident.' Appearance isn't everything, but well-chosen sneakers can contribute to a polished first impression.

Social media perspective. When posting photos: more compliments, more 'Where'd you get those?', and better engagement because the overall outfit looks intentional. The customer begins building an identity around being stylish rather than simply owning shoes.

The deeper status transformation. The sneaker isn't really selling rubber, leather, or foam. It's selling movement up a social ladder.

The customer moves from: looking average to looking stylish, feeling overlooked to feeling noticed, feeling unsure to feeling confident, following trends to being someone others ask for recommendations, wanting approval to receiving compliments naturally.

The identity shift. The most valuable transformation is psychological.

Before: 'I hope nobody notices my shoes.' After: 'I hope people notice my shoes.' Eventually: 'I don't even have to think about it anymore—I know I look good.'

That quiet confidence changes how they walk, stand, interact, and present themselves. People often respond to that confidence as much as they respond to the sneakers themselves.

Example 2: Luxury Handbags. The customer's internal perspective: 'I feel elegant the moment I carry it.' 'I finally look like the woman I've always imagined myself becoming.' 'Everything about my outfit feels more polished.' 'I walk into meetings, events, and restaurants with more confidence.' 'I'm no longer trying to fit in—I feel like I belong.' 'This isn't just a bag. It's a reflection of how far I've come.' 'I feel feminine, sophisticated, and intentional.' 'I worked hard for this. Every time I carry it, I'm reminded of my success.'

The handbag becomes an extension of her identity. She doesn't just own a luxury bag—she feels like a woman who appreciates quality and has refined taste.

Friends' perspective. Her friends might think: 'She always looks so classy.' 'Her style has really evolved.' 'She's becoming successful.' 'She has expensive taste.' 'She always knows what's fashionable.' They may ask: 'Where did you buy that?' 'Can I see your bag?' 'I've been looking for something like that.' Instead of simply admiring the handbag, they're admiring the woman carrying it.

Family's perspective. Parents may think: 'She's doing well for herself.' 'She's become so confident.' 'She carries herself differently now.' Siblings may think: 'She's really leveled up.' Extended family members might notice: 'She always looks elegant.' The handbag becomes visible evidence that she's investing in herself and enjoying the rewards of her hard work.

Colleagues' perspective. Coworkers may think: 'She's very put together.' 'She has excellent taste.' 'She pays attention to details.' 'She presents herself professionally.' Without saying a word, the handbag contributes to an image of confidence, competence, and sophistication.

Other women's perspective. Women often notice handbags before almost anything else. They may think: 'That bag is gorgeous.' 'She has amazing style.' 'She clearly knows quality.' 'I'd love one like that.' 'I'm so jealous of her.' In many social settings, a luxury handbag quietly communicates fashion awareness and discernment.

Men's perspective. Many men won't recognize the specific brand, but they'll notice the overall impression. They may think: 'She's elegant.' 'She looks sophisticated.' 'She takes good care of herself.' 'She has confidence.' The handbag contributes to an overall polished appearance rather than standing out on its own.

Strangers' perspective. Walking into a hotel, café, airport, or upscale event, people may assume: 'She's successful.' 'She has refined taste.' 'She belongs here.' Whether or not those assumptions are accurate, luxury accessories often shape first impressions.

Social media perspective. Photos instantly look more polished. Followers may comment: 'You look amazing.' 'Love your style.' 'That bag is everything.' 'You're glowing.' The handbag enhances the overall aesthetic and reinforces a consistent image of elegance.

The emotional transformation. Before: 'I hope I look good enough.' After: 'I know I've put my best foot forward.' Before: 'I don't feel completely put together.' After: 'Every detail reflects who I am.' Before: 'I'm trying to look elegant.' After: 'I feel elegant.'

The status transformation. The handbag helps shift how the customer sees herself and how others respond to her.

She goes from: carrying a bag to carrying herself with confidence, dressing nicely to looking effortlessly elegant, following fashion to expressing personal style, blending into the crowd to leaving a memorable impression, wanting validation to feeling self-assured regardless of others' opinions.

The identity she's really buying. Ultimately, she's not buying leather, stitching, or hardware.

She's buying the feeling of becoming: a woman with refined taste, a woman who values quality over quantity, a woman who celebrates her achievements, a woman who appears polished and intentional, a woman who commands respect through quiet confidence, a woman who feels as successful on the outside as she strives to be on the inside.

Now do you see the real products being sold? And why top brands keep printing money with overpriced items?

The experience. The status. That's the commodity.

This is where you become an artist. Every single line from those ChatGPT responses is a potential advertising angle. You don't need to use all of them.

Pick one. Maybe you want your customer to imagine how their friends will react. Maybe you want to focus on how they'll feel walking into a room. Maybe you want to highlight the impression they'll leave on colleagues or strangers.

Whatever perspective you choose, your job as a marketer is to bring that feeling to life. Instead of advertising the product itself, depict the transformation in how your customer sees themselves—or how they believe others will see them.

That's what great marketing does.

Understand the system, not just the prompt. It's easy to think the secret here is the ChatGPT prompt. It isn't.

The prompt is simply a tool. The real lesson is understanding the system behind it.

Whenever you're marketing a product that doesn't solve an obvious, painful problem, ask yourself: What social outcome is my customer really buying?

That's the question the prompt forces you to answer. Once you understand that, you'll start seeing marketing angles everywhere. You'll stop describing products and start selling identity, confidence, belonging, admiration, professionalism, elegance, and status—the things your customers actually imagine when they picture themselves owning your product.

Don't copy the tactic. Understand the system. Because once you understand the system, you can create winning marketing angles long after you've forgotten the prompt.

Your turn. Copy the prompt above. Replace [your product] with whatever you sell. In less than 30 seconds, you'll likely discover five or more advertising angles you weren't using before.

categories.name: Networking Systems
categories.description: Turning other creators and businesses into allies instead of competition.

articles.title: The Clique Method
articles.excerpt: Everyone chasing followers is playing the game wrong. Here’s the simple networking system that turns other creators into referral partners instead of competition.
articles.content:
There's an old saying: 'No man is an island.' It applies to every aspect of life, but it's never been more true than when you're trying to build a business online. One of the biggest mistakes entrepreneurs make is treating everyone in their space as competition.

By the end of this article, you'll learn a simple networking system that can generate opportunities, referrals, collaborations, and even customers—without spending a single dollar on advertising.

Stop seeing competitors. Start seeing prospects. When you come across another creator or business in your industry, your first instinct might be to compare yourself. They have more followers. Better content. More engagement. More customers.

But that's the wrong way to look at them. You see, every creator or business is missing something or has room to improve somewhere. In the same way, you're missing things too, and there are places where you can improve.

One might be great at graphic design. Another writes incredible copy. Someone else has a loyal email list. Another has strong sales skills. Instead of competing with all of them individually, imagine combining those strengths. That's where the real leverage begins.

The Clique Method. Here's the system.

1. Find your lane. Don't obsess over finding others in your exact niche. Instead, seek people who speak the same language and are chasing similar goals. If you help small businesses grow… talk to marketers, copywriters, designers, web developers, business coaches, and agencies. You don't have to sell the same thing. You just need enough overlap that helping one another makes sense.

2. Engage first. Before you ever ask for anything, show up on their posts (a few comments are okay so long as they're replying—but the more times you engage, the better). Become familiar before becoming memorable. People are naturally more receptive to someone they've seen contributing consistently than someone whose first interaction is a pitch.

3. Pitch the clique. Once you've built some familiarity by engaging with and commenting on their posts, send the DM. Keep it simple and straightforward. Something like: 'I've noticed a few of us are all trying to solve the same problem—growing attention for our businesses. What do you think about creating a small group where we share ideas, referrals, and opportunities?' Notice how you're not pitching yourself? You're pitching a shared benefit.

4. Expect rejection. Some won't reply. Some won't be interested. Some will join and disappear. That's normal. From personal experience, around 30% of those who join remain active—and that's enough, depending on how many people you reached out to. You don't need fifty people. You only need a handful who genuinely participate. Five committed people will outperform fifty inactive members every time.

5. Become the glue. This is hands-down the most important part. Talk to members individually. You'll notice the group chat will mostly consist of surface-level interactions—sharing posts, and so on. That's why you need to build rapport with each active member.

Learn what they're good at (beyond the obvious). Find out what they need. Then start connecting people. Recommend the designer to the copywriter. Recommend the copywriter to the agency owner. Recommend someone's service when another member needs it. Bundle a partner's offer with your own—or with another partner's—if it genuinely creates more value for the customer. The more opportunities you create between other people, the more valuable you become to the entire group.

Why this works. A small clique creates accountability, trust, referrals, partnerships, and opportunities that simply don't exist when everyone is working alone. Instead of each person trying to solve every problem themselves, the group begins sharing resources, introductions, expertise, and even customers. The result isn't just faster growth; it's leveraged growth.

Don't just copy the tactic. Understand the SYSTEM. The lesson isn't 'start a networking group.' It's that every business has gaps and room for improvement. So instead of trying to fill every gap yourself, build systems that connect you with people whose strengths complement yours. That's leverage. The connector isn't always the smartest person in the room, but they're often the most valuable.

Your turn. Find three creators or businesses operating in your lane today. Don't pitch them. Engage with them. Start genuine conversations. Then, when the opportunity is right, invite them to build a small clique around a shared goal. You'll be surprised how quickly opportunities begin finding you instead of you chasing them.

categories.name: Mindset and Others
categories.description: Perspective shifts, myths, and the occasional detour from the playbook.

articles.title: Why Do Men-Led Businesses End Up More Successful Than Women-Led Businesses?
articles.excerpt: Men-led businesses statistically generate more revenue than women-led ones. The data says the gap isn’t about performance — it’s about who gets funded.
articles.content:
Statistically, men-led businesses often end up generating more revenue than women-led businesses. Why is that? Many people assume they already know the answer. Are men inherently better entrepreneurs? Are men naturally smarter? Are men bigger risk-takers? Do men start more businesses? Spoiler alert: not anymore.

By the end of this article, you'll understand what the research actually says about this gap. First of all, let's swing a wrecking ball at those stereotypes. Because the data tells a very different story.

As of 2024, women started 49% of new businesses in the United States—nearly identical to men and a dramatic increase from just 29% in 2019. Some recent surveys even suggest women have been launching businesses at a slightly higher rate than men.

It gets even more interesting. Research from Boston Consulting Group found that female-founded startups generated more revenue per dollar invested than male-founded startups. Think about that for a moment. Women are starting businesses at about the same rate as men, and research suggests they often generate a higher return on each investment dollar.

So why do male-led businesses still tend to become bigger, raise more capital, and ultimately generate more total revenue?

The answer isn't performance. The biggest difference isn't actually business performance. It's access to funding. In 2024, all-female founding teams received just 2.3% of the $289 billion in global venture capital funding. All-male founding teams received 83.6%.

That funding gap has real consequences. Average annual revenue for women-owned businesses is estimated at roughly $219,000–$226,000, while male-owned businesses average approximately $474,000–$607,000. Let that sink in.

Why does the funding gap exist? Researchers have identified several factors that appear to contribute to this imbalance.

1. Venture capital networks remain overwhelmingly male. Most venture capital decision-makers are still men, with women making up only about 17–18% of VC decision-makers. Like many industries, investors often build relationships with—and invest in—people who resemble themselves or come from similar networks.

2. Pitch evaluations aren't always objective. Multiple studies have documented bias during investment pitches. One study found that investors rated identical startup pitches as more persuasive and more evidence-based when they were presented using a male voice. Another found that investors often ask male and female founders different kinds of questions, with the questions directed at men tending to position them more favorably.

3. The stereotype of a 'fundable founder.' Many investors unconsciously associate successful founders with traits such as dominance, aggression, and bold risk-taking. Whether or not those traits actually predict entrepreneurial success, they continue to influence perceptions of who 'looks' like a successful founder.

4. Women-focused markets are often underestimated. Products aimed primarily at women have sometimes been dismissed as serving 'niche' markets, despite representing enormous commercial opportunities. When investors underestimate the size of these markets, promising businesses can struggle to secure funding.

5. Failure is judged differently. This one's interesting. A Yale study found that after one unsuccessful investment in a woman-led startup, investors became less likely to fund women-led businesses again. However, a successful investment in a woman-led company didn't make those same investors more likely to invest in women in the future. Researchers did not observe the same pattern with male founders.

6. Attribution bias makes the problem harder to solve. Even attempts to reduce the funding gap can encounter another obstacle. Research suggests that when female founders receive funding exclusively from female investors, outside observers may attribute that success to solidarity rather than merit. Ironically, this perception can make raising future investment even more difficult.

The real lesson. It's easy to look at larger companies and assume the biggest businesses simply have the best founders. But business growth isn't determined by ability alone. Access to capital plays an enormous role in how quickly a company can hire, market, expand, and compete. That's why it's important to distinguish between business performance and business funding. They're related—but they're not the same thing.

Your turn. The next time someone argues that men are simply better at business because male-led companies tend to be larger, remember this: women are starting businesses at nearly the same rate as men, and some research suggests they generate higher returns per investment dollar. The biggest gap is in who receives the funding needed to scale them.
